column1: # Principle: Strict left-to-right evaluation when chaining same-level
# forward-collecting words.
# When two (or more) forward-collecting words are at the same syntactic
# level (i.e. neither is in the other's argument slot), the engine
# evaluates them strictly left-to-right. Each word collects its
# arguments before the next word executes.
# This is distinct from "nested forward" (which would let an inner
# function fire inside an outer's arg slot — see nested-forward.tsv:
# that case ERRORS in aqleng without explicit parens). Left-to-right
# is what powers the "infix-looking" reader: `2 addq 3 mulq 4` reads
# as `(2 + 3) * 4 = 20` because `addq` fires first, leaving 5 on the
# stack, then `mulq` collects 4 forward and finds 5 on the stack.
# Rules being asserted
# 1. `a OP1 b OP2 c` evaluates as `(a OP1 b) OP2 c` when both OPs are
#    2-arg forward-prec words. The first OP's result lives on the
#    stack and becomes the second OP's stack-side arg.
# 2. The chain is unbounded: `1 addq 2 addq 3 addq 4` etc.
# 3. Mixing operators with different precedence is irrelevant — there
#    are no precedence levels in aqleng. Strictly left-to-right.
# 4. Parens DO override the default left-to-right reading. `2 addq (3
#    mulq 4)` runs the mulq first, producing 12, then addq(2, 12) = 14.
# 5. Forward-eligible-with-stack-fallback words (e.g. `negq`)
#    interleave fine — they fire when the engine reaches them, taking
#    their arg from forward if available, otherwise from the stack.
# 6. Using a forward-prec word in a position where the next thing is
#    another forward-prec word is OK as long as the second word
#    DOESN'T need the first as an arg — the second's prefix slot is
#    just whatever the first leaves on the stack.
# Why this matters
# AQL has no operator precedence table — the language picks the
# uniform rule "left to right, with parens for override". This avoids
# the entire precedence-table category of design questions that other
# languages spend tooling on (associativity / parentheses-suggester /
# precedence-fix-it lints). The test rows here pin down the rule:
# breaking left-to-right would change the outcome of essentially
# every multi-operator expression.
# === Test fixtures (defined in aqleng/go/spec_test.go::registerSpecWords) ===
#   addq  — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   mulq  — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] * args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   negq  — [Number|] BarrierPos=1 — unary -args[0]; preserves Integer/Decimal tag
#   subq  — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] - args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   Pre-bound simple-value defs: pi=3 (Integer), tau=6 (Integer), greeting="hello" (String).
# === end fixtures ===
# tokens
# Two-op chains.
2 addq 3 mulq 4
2 mulq 3 addq 4
1 addq 2 addq 3
10 subq 3 subq 2
column3: first subq: stk=[10], fwd=[3] → sig=[3,10] → 10-3=7 on stack; second subq: stk=[7], fwd=[2] → sig=[2,7] → 7-2=5

column1: # Three- and four-op chains.
1 addq 2 mulq 3 addq 4
1 addq 2 addq 3 addq 4
2 mulq 3 mulq 4
1 addq 2 mulq 3 mulq 4
# Parens override the default order.
2 addq ( 3 mulq 4 )
( 2 addq 3 ) mulq 4
column3: addq runs first (paren); 5 mulq 4 = 20 — same as bare chain

column1: 1 addq ( 2 mulq ( 3 addq 4 ) )
( 1 addq 2 ) mulq ( 3 addq 4 )
# Unary numeric word in the chain. negq is forward-eligible with
# stack fallback, so `5 negq` consumes the stack value when there's
# nothing after to fwd-collect.
5 negq addq 1
column3: 5 negq=-5 (fwd empty, stack supplies); addq fwd 1 with stack -5; sig=[1,-5] → 1+-5=-4

column1: 1 addq 5 negq
column3: addq fwd-scans 5, hits negq as function-word boundary; addq(5,1)=6 (sig[0]=5 fwd, sig[1]=1 stack); negq then consumes 6 → -6

column1: 3 negq negq
column3: double negation cancels — first negq consumes 3 from stack (boundary on second negq), second negq consumes -3

column1: # Mixing with simple-value def substitution. pi=3, tau=6 are pre-bound.
pi addq tau mulq 2
pi mulq tau
column3: 3*6 = 18 — both args from def (mirror form: mulq 6 3 = 18, but commutative so same)

column1: # Mixed prefix-then-mid-forward: a forward-prec word can pull one
# arg from a future token AND one from the stack — but the stack
# value used is the TOP, not the deepest. A pre-existing residue
# below the top is preserved unchanged.
1 2 addq 3 mulq
column3: addq: fwd=3, stk top=2 → sig[0]=3, sig[1]=2, addq(3,2)=5; mulq then runs with stack=[1,5], top=5, deeper=1; sig[0]=5, sig[1]=1, mulq=5*1=5. The 1 from the original prefix is consumed by mulq, not preserved.

column1: 1 2 addq 4 mulq
column3: identical to above with different forward arg; final mulq=6*1=6

column1: 0 1 2 addq
column3: the 0 below 1 stays as residue; addq consumes 1 and 2 only
